text: Hello everyone, my name is Dr. Sanket Pissatt and I am a consultant gynecological minimal access surgeon practicing in Mumbai, India.
The video displays the anatomy of the female reproductive system, highlighting the central position of the uterus with the fallopian tubes and ovaries located symmetrically on either side.
The video shows the anatomical relationship of the uterus, positioned anteriorly to the urinary bladder and posteriorly to the rectum.

So there are several important vital structures which are lying in close relation to the uterus which have to be remembered during laparoscopic hysterectomy which is also called uterus removal surgery.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by dividing the corneal support structures at the top of the uterus using electrocautery. The same technique is applied to divide the corneal support structures on the opposite side.
The surgeon divides the peritoneal covering surrounding the uterus, preparing for its removal.
The surgeon divides the peritoneal covering, known as the broad ligament, on the side of the uterus using surgical instruments.
The surgeon carefully separates the uterus from the urinary bladder, which is positioned anteriorly to the uterus, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon carefully separates the urinary bladder from the anterior surface of the uterus, ensuring that the bladder is not damaged during the hysterectomy procedure.
The urinary bladder is being carefully separated from the anterior surface of the uterus using laparoscopic instruments, preparing for the continuation of the hysterectomy procedure.
Following the separation of the urinary bladder, the surgeon proceeds to divide the main blood supply channel to the uterus, preparing for its removal.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery, the main blood supply channel to the uterus, preparing for its division as part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the uterine artery on the left side, which serves as the main blood supply channel to the uterus, and performs a similar procedure on the right side.
Following the division of the uterine artery, the surgeon incises the vagina or separates the cervix from the vaginal tissue, preparing for the removal of the uterus.
The uterus and cervix have been separated, revealing the underlying vaginal tissue.
The surgeon prepares to remove the fallopian tubes and ovaries, following the previous separation of the uterus and cervix from surrounding structures.

Generally, in case of a young patient who is less than fifty years of age, the ovaries need not be removed because they keep continuing to secrete estrogen hormone which lasts for a long time till about fifty to fifty-five years of age.
The surgeon removes the fallopian tubes, ensuring they are completely excised to prevent potential swelling in the future.
The two removed fallopian tubes are visible. The surgeon sutures the vagina using absorbable sutures for vaginal reconstruction, restoring its normal anatomical position.
